concepts.title: Why the Croatian alphabet is your superpower
concepts.simpleExplanation: Croatian spelling is perfectly phonetic: every letter is always pronounced the same way, and every sound is always written the same way. There are no silent letters and no surprises. That means once you've learned the 30 letters — especially the special ones like č, ć, š, ž, dž, đ, lj and nj — you can correctly read aloud ANY Croatian word you've never seen, and you can spell any word you hear. English speakers spend years on spelling; in Croatian you solve it in a week.
concepts.analogy: It's like a piano where each key always plays the same note. Learn the keys once and you can play any sheet of music on sight.

rubricPoints.reTeach: Each letter always makes the same sound, and each sound is always written the same way.
Unlike French or English, you pronounce everything you see.
These carry sounds like ch, sh, zh, ly, ny — learn them and you've cracked pronunciation.

rubricPoints.point: Once learned, you can read AND spell any word.
rubricPoints.reTeach: Reading aloud and spelling-from-listening both become automatic.

concepts.title: Why Croatian has no 'a' or 'the'
concepts.simpleExplanation: Croatian simply doesn't have articles. There is no word for 'a' and no word for 'the'. A noun like 'kuća' can mean 'a house', 'the house', or just 'house' depending on context. This makes sentences shorter, but it means you have to rely on the situation (and sometimes word order or demonstratives like 'ovaj' = this) to know which English article fits when translating.
concepts.analogy: Imagine English if you just said 'I see house' and let the conversation make clear whether it's a specific house or any house. That's Croatian.

rubricPoints.reTeach: Croatian has zero articles — none at all.
'kuća' = a house / the house / house, depending on context.
If you need to say 'this/that house', use demonstratives like ovaj/taj.

concepts.title: What grammatical cases actually do
concepts.simpleExplanation: In Croatian, the ENDING of a noun changes depending on the job it does in the sentence. These different endings are called cases. The nominative is the dictionary form and marks the subject (the one doing the action). The accusative marks the direct object (the thing receiving the action). So 'žena' (woman, subject) drinks 'kavu' (coffee, object) — 'kava' changed its ending to 'kavu' to show it's the object. Because the ending tells you each word's role, word order can move around without losing the meaning. Croatian has 7 cases, but nominative and accusative alone handle most basic sentences.
concepts.analogy: Cases are like name tags that say 'I'm the subject' or 'I'm the object'. Once everyone wears a tag, they can stand anywhere in the room and you still know who's who.

rubricPoints.point: A case is shown by the noun's ENDING.
rubricPoints.reTeach: The end of the word changes to show its role — not a separate word, the ending itself.
The doer of the action, and the form you look up: 'žena'.
The receiver of the action: 'kava' → 'kavu' in 'Pijem kavu'.

rubricPoints.point: Because endings show role, word order is flexible.
rubricPoints.reTeach: You can rearrange words and the case endings keep the meaning clear.

rubricPoints.point: There are 7 cases; start with just nominative + accusative.
rubricPoints.reTeach: Don't learn all 7 at once — these two cover most basic sentences.

concepts.title: Verb aspect: ongoing vs completed
concepts.simpleExplanation: Most Croatian verbs come in pairs that describe the SAME action but from two viewpoints. The imperfective verb views the action as ongoing, repeated, or a process ('I was drinking', 'I drink every day'). The perfective verb views it as a single, completed whole ('I drank it all up'). For example, 'piti' is imperfective and 'popiti' is perfective. Often a prefix (like po-, na-, pro-) turns an imperfective verb into its perfective partner. Aspect replaces a lot of the work English does with different tenses.
concepts.analogy: Imperfective is a video of the action playing; perfective is a photo of it finished. Same event, different lens.

rubricPoints.reTeach: Two verbs describe the same action from different viewpoints.
Use it for actions in progress or habits: 'I was reading', 'I read daily'.
Use it for a finished whole: 'I read (and finished) the book'.

rubricPoints.point: A prefix often makes a verb perfective.
rubricPoints.reTeach: piti → popiti, pisati → napisati: the prefix signals completion.

concepts.title: How the past tense (perfekt) works
concepts.simpleExplanation: The everyday Croatian past tense is built from two pieces: the short form of biti ('to be' — sam, si, je, smo, ste, su) plus a special past participle of the main verb. The participle changes its ending to agree with the speaker's gender and number: -o for masculine, -la for feminine, -lo for neuter, -li/-le for plural. So a man says 'Radio sam' (I worked) and a woman says 'Radila sam'. To make it negative, you swap to the negative biti: 'Nisam radio' (I didn't work).
concepts.analogy: It's like English 'I have worked', but the 'worked' part puts on a different outfit depending on whether a man, woman, or group is speaking.

rubricPoints.reTeach: Two pieces: sam/si/je... plus the participle, e.g. 'sam' + 'radio'.

rubricPoints.point: The participle agrees with gender and number.
rubricPoints.reTeach: -o (m), -la (f), -lo (n), -li/-le (pl).
The same action takes a different participle ending by speaker gender.
'Nisam radio' = I didn't work — swap the helper to its negative form.
